headline: African Wild Dogs Return to Malawi After 10-Year Absence
meta_description: Four endangered African wild dogs spotted in Kasungu National Park, the first sighting in over a decade, signaling ecosystem recovery and wildlife corridor suc…
summary_short: Four endangered African wild dogs have been spotted in Malawi's Kasungu National Park for the first time in over 10 years, marking a major conservation victory.
body:
Four endangered African wild dogs appeared in Kasungu National Park in Malawi on July 17, marking the first confirmed sighting of the species there in more than a decade. Using photo identification and a long-term database, researchers from the Zambian Carnivore Programme recognized the animals from South Luangwa National Park in Zambia—roughly 300 kilometers away—where they had been recorded near Mfuwe village just two months earlier. The dogs' 186-mile journey demonstrates that wildlife can traverse the newly connected landscape between the two countries, even through areas densely packed with villages and farms. Gervaz Thamala, field operations manager for the International Fund for Animal Welfare, photographed the four dogs near the park's eastern boundary, describing them as "so relaxed" as they rested a few hundred meters from the main gate.

The sighting reveals something deeper than a lucky encounter: it shows that years of restoration work are bearing fruit. In 2022, the Malawian government, IFAW, and African Parks restocked Kasungu with hundreds of animals, including impalas—a preferred prey species for wild dogs. Those impalas now thrive in the southern section of the park, near where the dogs were spotted, creating conditions that could support a permanent pack. Former den sites in both the south and northwest of the park, dormant for years, may soon host families again.

Mathew Becker, chief executive of the Zambian Carnivore Programme, called the arrival "very promising," suggesting it signals future exchange of wild dogs between Zambia and Malawi through natural dispersal. Bruce Sosola, IFAW Malawi's country director, framed the return as proof of healing: "The natural return of African wild dogs to Kasungu National Park after a decade isn't just an extraordinary sighting, it's proof that our ecosystem is truly healing."
quick_facts: Four African wild dogs spotted in Kasungu National Park on July 17
First sighting of the species in the park in over 10 years
Dogs traveled 300 km from South Luangwa National Park in Zambia
Park restocked with impalas in 2022 as prey for wild dogs
Sighting signals success of wildlife corridor between two countries
